lessons.title: Generics Basics
lessons.summary: Learn the purpose and basic syntax of generics, and understand how to write type-safe code.

contents.title: What are Generics?
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- The purpose of **generics** (improving **type safety**)
- Declaring and using **type parameters** <T>
- The dangers of [[casting]] without [[generics]]

By parameterizing types, bugs can be detected at [[compile]] time.

contents.title: Defining a Generic Class
contents.body:
A generic [[class]] is defined by declaring a **type parameter** in angle brackets (<>) after the class name.
The type parameter can be used as the type for fields, [[method]] arguments, and return values within the class.
When creating an [[instance]], you specify a concrete type, causing the class to behave as if specialized for that type.
Since Java 7, the [[diamond operator]] (<>) can be used to omit the type argument on the right side.
Only [[reference types]] can be specified as type parameters; [[primitive types]] like int and double cannot be used.

contents.title: Type Parameter Naming Conventions
contents.body:
By convention, single uppercase letters are used for type parameters.
T (Type) represents a general type and is the most widely used.
E (Element) is used for collection element types (e.g., List<E>).
K (Key) and V (Value) are used for map keys and values (e.g., Map<K, V>).
N (Number) is sometimes used for numeric types.
Classes with multiple type parameters can also be defined, declared separated by commas.

contents.title: Benefits of Type Safety and No Casting
contents.body:
Without **generics**, any object can be stored in a collection.
When retrieving elements, explicit casting is required, and casting to the wrong type results in a runtime [[exception]].
With generics, storing an invalid type is detected at compile time.
This prevents runtime [[ClassCastException]] and enables safe code.
[[Type inference]] also reduces verbose code and improves code readability.

contents.title: Notes on Raw Types
contents.body:
Using a generic class without specifying the type parameter is called a raw type.
Using raw types causes compile-time warnings (unchecked warning).
Adding elements to a raw type collection loses **type safety**.
On the Java Gold exam, questions about mixing raw types and generic types may appear.
Raw types are permitted for compatibility with existing legacy code, but always specify type parameters in new code.
contents.note: Raw types will compile, but since type safety is not guaranteed, they should be avoided as a rule.

quizzes.question: Which of the following is a primary benefit of using generics?
quizzes.explanation: The primary benefit of generics is ensuring type safety at compile time. This causes invalid type assignments to result in compile errors, preventing ClassCastException at runtime. Improved execution speed and reduced memory usage are not direct benefits of generics.

choices.text: Primitive types can be directly stored in collections

quizzes.explanation: A generic class's type parameter is defined at class declaration and used as the type for fields, method arguments, and return values. By convention, T is used as a general type parameter. The same type parameter T is specified in all blanks.

quizzes.question: Which of the following code snippets will cause a compile error?
quizzes.explanation: Primitive types (int, double, char, etc.) cannot be specified as generic type parameters. Only reference types (Integer, String, Object, etc.) can be used. Therefore, Box<int> causes a compile error. If you need to use a primitive type, use the corresponding wrapper class (Integer, Double, etc.).

lessons.title: Generic Methods
lessons.summary: Learn how to define generic methods that declare type parameters at the method level, and understand type inference.

contents.title: What are Generic Methods?
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Syntax for defining generic [[methods]]
- The difference between [[class]] **type parameters** and method type parameters
- Utilizing the [[diamond operator]] <> with [[type inference]]

A mechanism that allows flexible type specification at the method level.

contents.title: Defining a Generic Method
contents.body:
To define a generic method, declare the **type parameter** in angle brackets immediately before the return type.
The declared type parameter can be used as the type for that method's arguments, return value, and local [[variables]].
Method type parameters are independent of class type parameters, and even if they have the same name, they are treated as separate type parameters.
Generic methods increase code reusability and allow the same logic to be applied to different types.
contents.note: The "//" in the code indicates a comment (explanatory text). It does not affect program execution.

contents.title: How Type Inference Works
contents.body:
When calling a generic method, in most cases, you don't need to explicitly specify the type parameter.
The compiler infers the concrete type of the type parameter from the argument types (Type Inference).
To explicitly specify the type, write the dot [[operator]] and type argument before the method name.
For [[static methods]], use the format ClassName.<Type>methodName.
Since Java 8, type inference capabilities have been enhanced, and explicit type specification is unnecessary in more cases.

contents.title: Static Methods and Type Parameters
contents.body:
Static methods cannot access the class's type parameters.
This is because class type parameters are tied to [[instances]], and static methods do not depend on instances.
Therefore, when you want to use **generics** in a static method, you must declare the method's own type parameter.
This point is frequently tested on the Java Gold exam.
contents.note: Attempting to use the class's type parameter in a static method will cause a [[compile]] error. Always declare the method's own type parameter.

contents.title: Use Cases for Generic Methods
contents.body:
Generic methods are particularly useful in utility classes.
They are widely used in the standard library, such as the [[Collections]] class's sort() and emptyList().
They are suitable when you want to create a relationship between method argument types and return types.
For example, they can be used for methods that return a value of the same type as the input, or methods that guarantee two arguments are of the same type.

quizzes.question: What is the output when the following code is executed?
quizzes.explanation: The pickFirst method returns the first of the two arguments. The type parameter T is inferred as String. pickFirst("Hello", "World") returns the first argument "Hello", so the output is "Hello".

quizzes.question: Which of the following will cause a compile error?
quizzes.explanation: The class type parameter T cannot be used in static methods. Method C uses the class type parameter T in a static method, causing a compile error. As in B, declaring the method's own type parameter <U> allows generics to be used in static methods.

quizzes.question:
Choose the correct syntax for invoking a generic method with an explicit type specification.

List<String> list = Util.___singletonList("A");
quizzes.explanation: When calling a static generic method with an explicit type specification, use the format ClassName.<Type>methodName. Util.<String>singletonList("A") is the correct syntax. The type argument is written after the dot operator and before the method name.

quizzes.question: Complete the definition of the generic class.
quizzes.explanation: Generics declare a type parameter with <T> and use T as a type within the class. Type safety is guaranteed at compile time.

lessons.title: Bounded Type Parameters
lessons.summary: Learn how to use bounded type parameters to impose constraints on type parameters.

contents.title: What are Bounded Type Parameters?
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Setting an upper bound on **type parameters** with extends
- Enabling [[method]] calls with bounded type parameters
- Multiple type bounds (combining with &)

A way to add the constraint "must be at least this type" to type parameters.

contents.title: Using Upper Bounds
contents.body:
An upper bound is specified in the format <T extends UpperBoundType>.
Setting an upper bound restricts the **type parameter** T to subtypes of the specified type.
This allows methods defined in the upper bound type to be safely called on variables of type T.
Not only [[classes]] but also [[interfaces]] can be specified as upper bounds.
contents.note: The "//" in the code indicates a comment (explanatory text). It does not affect program execution.

contents.title: Multiple Bounds
contents.body:
To set multiple bounds on a type parameter, separate them with & (ampersand).
Multiple bounds require the type parameter to satisfy multiple types simultaneously.
When combining classes and interfaces, the class must be listed first.
When only interfaces are used, there is no ordering constraint, but at most one class can be specified.
Multiple bounds allow expressing more strict type constraints.
contents.note: When using multiple bounds with both a class and interfaces, the class must always be listed first.

contents.title: Recursive Type Bounds
contents.body:
A recursive type bound is a pattern where the type parameter has a bound that references itself.
A typical example is <T extends [[Comparable]]<T>>, which ensures that T types can be compared with each other.
This pattern is frequently used in sorting and computing the maximum/minimum values of collections.
In the Java standard API, the Enum class also uses a recursive type bound: Enum<E extends Enum<E>>.
Understanding this pattern may be tested on the Gold exam.

contents.title: Differences Between Bounded Type Parameters and Wildcards
contents.body:
Bounded type parameters (<T extends X>) and **wildcards** (<? extends X>) are similar but serve different roles.
Bounded type parameters give the type a name (T), so it can be referenced as the same type within the method.
[[Wildcards]] have no name, so they cannot be referenced as the same type in multiple places.
Use bounded type parameters when you need to relate the return type with multiple argument types.
If you simply need to read elements from a collection, wildcards are simpler.

quizzes.question:
Which is the correct type parameter declaration for the following generic method?
"T must be a subclass of Number and implement Comparable"
quizzes.explanation: When specifying multiple bounds, separate them with &. When mixing classes and interfaces, the rule is that the class must come first. Therefore, <T extends Number & Comparable<T>> is the correct notation. Comma-separated notation or putting the interface first will cause compile errors.

quizzes.question: The following code has a compile error. What is the cause of the error?
quizzes.explanation: Since no bound is specified for the type parameter T, T is treated as Object type. The Object type does not have a doubleValue() method, so it causes a compile error. By setting an upper bound like <T extends Number>, you can call Number class's doubleValue().

choices.text: No bound is specified for T, so doubleValue() cannot be called
Generic types cannot be used in for-each statements
Generics cannot be used in static methods
The return type double is incompatible with T

quizzes.question: What is the output when the following code is executed?
quizzes.explanation: The max method returns the larger of the two arguments. In max(3, 7), they are compared as Integer type, and 7 is larger so 7 is returned. In max("Apple", "Banana"), they are compared lexicographically as String type, and "Banana" is larger so "Banana" is returned.

quizzes.question: Select all correct statements about generic type parameter bounds.
quizzes.explanation: The upper bound (extends) is for read-only (Producer), and the lower bound (super) is for write-only (Consumer), following the PECS principle. The wildcard <?> is an unbounded type that accepts any type but cannot add elements. Multiple upper bounds can be specified with one class plus multiple interfaces.

choices.text: <? extends T> is an upper bound and is suited for reading
<? super T> is a lower bound and is suited for writing
<?> can add any value other than null
Multiple upper bounds can be specified with &

quizzes.question: Select all correct statements about generics restrictions.
quizzes.explanation: Due to type erasure, generics have the following restrictions: primitive types are not allowed, new T() is not allowed, and the class type parameter T cannot be used in static contexts. There is no limit on the number of type parameters.

choices.text: Primitive types cannot be used as type parameters
Instances of a type parameter cannot be created with new
Type parameters cannot be used in static fields
A maximum of 3 type parameters is allowed

lessons.title: Wildcards
lessons.summary: Learn the types and usage of wildcards (?), and the PECS principle.

contents.title: Overview of Wildcards
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- The basic concept of **wildcards** (?)
- How to use upper bounded [[wildcards]] (? extends T)
- How to use lower bounded wildcards (? super T)

Let's understand the PECS principle (Producer-Extends, [[Consumer]]-Super).

contents.title: Unbounded and Upper Bounded Wildcards
contents.body:
The unbounded **wildcard** <?> means accepting any type.
Since the element type is unknown, no elements can be added to the list except null.
The upper bounded wildcard <? extends T> accepts collections containing elements of type T or its subtypes.
Elements can be read from a list with an upper bounded wildcard, but no elements can be added (except null).
contents.note: The "//" in the code indicates a comment (explanatory text). It does not affect program execution.

contents.title: Lower Bounded Wildcards
contents.body:
The lower bounded wildcard <? super T> accepts collections containing elements of type T or its supertypes.
Elements of type T and its subtypes can be added to a list with a lower bounded wildcard.
However, when reading elements, they can only be retrieved as Object type.
This is because the exact type of the elements is unknown.
contents.note: The "//" in the code indicates a comment (explanatory text). It does not affect program execution.

contents.title: The PECS Principle
contents.body:
PECS (Producer Extends, Consumer Super) is a principle for remembering how to use wildcards.
When using a collection as a Producer (retrieving data from it), use <? extends T>.
When using a collection as a Consumer (adding data to it), use <? super T>.
When both reading and writing are needed, don't use wildcards — specify the exact type.
This principle is typically demonstrated in the signature of the [[Collections]].copy() [[method]].
contents.note: PECS: Remember "extends for retrieving (Producer)" and "super for inserting (Consumer)".

contents.title: Wildcard Restrictions
contents.body:
Wildcards cannot be used in [[class]] or method **type parameter** declarations.
class MyClass<?> will cause a [[compile]] error.
Wildcards are only for use at "type usage sites", not at "type definition sites".
The fact that no elements other than null can be added to upper bounded wildcard lists requires special attention.
Through a mechanism called wildcard capture, the compiler may internally assign a temporary type to the wildcard.

quizzes.question: According to the PECS principle, which wildcard should be used when reading data from a collection (Produce)?
quizzes.explanation: Under the PECS principle, extends is used for a Producer (the side that retrieves data). Using <? extends T> allows safely reading elements of type T and its subtypes. super is used for a Consumer (the side that writes data).

quizzes.question: What happens when the following code is executed?
quizzes.explanation: List<Integer> can be assigned to List<? extends Number>. Since Integer is a subclass of Number, it can be read as Number type. intValue() is a method of the Number class and outputs the integer value of each element. The result is "10 20 30 ".

quizzes.question: The following code has a compile error. What is the cause of the error?
quizzes.explanation: No elements other than null can be added to a list with the upper bounded wildcard <? extends Number>. This is because the actual type of the list is unknown (whether it's List<Integer> or List<Double>), so type safety cannot be guaranteed. To add elements, use <? super Integer> instead.

choices.text: No elements except null can be added to a <? extends Number> list
The int literal 10 cannot be converted to Number type
List<Integer> cannot be assigned to List<? extends Number>
Wildcards cannot be used in static methods

quizzes.question: Arrange the elements of a generic method definition in the correct order.
quizzes.explanation: A generic method follows this order: access modifier → static → type parameter → return type and method name → body.

lessons.title: Type Erasure and Generics Restrictions
lessons.summary: Learn how Java generics are handled at runtime (type erasure) and the restrictions of generics.

contents.title: What is Type Erasure?
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- How **type erasure** (Type Erasure) works
- What cannot be done with **generics** (new T(), [[instanceof]] T, etc.)
- Why bridge [[methods]] are automatically generated

Let's understand the implications of [[generics]] information being erased after [[compilation]].

contents.title: How Type Erasure Works
contents.body:
The compiler transforms generic code into equivalent code without **type parameters** through **type erasure**.
Necessary [[casts]] are automatically inserted.
Bridge methods may be generated, which exist to maintain polymorphism in [[inheritance]] with generics.
The code after [[type erasure]] is compatible with Java code from before the introduction of generics.
contents.note: The "//" in the code indicates a comment (explanatory text). It does not affect program execution.

contents.title: Generics Restrictions
contents.body:
Understanding the main restrictions of generics due to type erasure is very important for the Java Gold exam.
[[Primitive types]] cannot be used as **type parameters** (Box<int> is not allowed; use Box<Integer>).
Type parameters cannot be used to create [[instances]] (new T() is not allowed).
Generic type [[arrays]] cannot be created directly (new T[] and new List<String>[] are not allowed).
The instanceof operator cannot be used with parameterized types (obj instanceof List<String> is not allowed).
The class type parameter cannot be used in [[static fields]] or [[static methods]].

contents.title: Generics and Overloading
contents.body:
Due to type erasure, methods that differ only in the generic type parameter cannot be [[overloaded]].
For example, method(List<String>) and method(List<Integer>) both become method(List) after type erasure, so they cannot coexist in the same class.
This causes a compile error.
You need to correctly understand the effects of type erasure and avoid method signature conflicts.
contents.note: Overloading where signatures become identical after type erasure will cause a compile error.

contents.title: Retaining Type Information with Type Tokens
contents.body:
While type parameter information is lost at runtime due to type erasure, a technique called type tokens can be used to retain type information.
By passing a Class object as an argument, type information can be used even at runtime.
This technique is widely used in frameworks and libraries.
Additionally, generic information of subclasses can sometimes be obtained via [[reflection]].

quizzes.question: Which statement correctly describes Java's type erasure?
quizzes.explanation: Java generics perform type checking only at compile time, and type parameter information is erased during bytecode generation. As a result, generic type parameter information does not exist at runtime. Unbounded type parameters are replaced with Object, and bounded type parameters are replaced with their bound type.

choices.text: Type checking is done at compile time, then type parameter info is erased at runtime
Type parameter information is retained at runtime
Type erasure significantly improves execution speed
Type erasure was deprecated in Java 11

quizzes.question: Which of the following code snippets will NOT cause a compile error?
quizzes.explanation: With the instanceof operator, parameterized types (like List<String>) cannot be used, but the unbounded wildcard (List<?>) is allowed. Due to type erasure, List<String> and List<Integer> are indistinguishable at runtime, so instanceof checks with parameterized types are meaningless. On the other hand, List<?> is allowed because it only checks "whether it's some kind of List".

quizzes.question: The following code has a compile error. What is the cause of the error?
quizzes.explanation: Due to type erasure, the concrete type information of T does not exist at runtime, so instances cannot be created with new T(). The compiler cannot resolve the constructor for type T, causing a compile error. As alternatives, you can receive a Class<T> object and create an instance via reflection, or use a Supplier<T>.

choices.text: Instance creation with new T() using a type parameter is not allowed
The type specification Factory<String> is invalid
Return type T cannot be assigned to a String variable
Generics cannot be used inside the main method
